author: Marc Elias
body:
The sudden falling out between Donald Trump and the Federalist Society is a ruse. Don’t be fooled. Trump is doing what he always does: creating a permission structure within the GOP to seize power and undermine the rule of law. We must not let him succeed.

It is no secret that Trump wants to be a dictator. Over the course of his first months in office, he used his position to claim powers he did not have, purge career civil servants and wage a campaign of retribution and intimidation against his opponents.

Standing in his way has been the courts. Federal judges have not blocked all his actions, but they have proven to be the biggest obstacle to his illegal and unconstitutional power grab. In May alone, Trump and his administration lost over 95% of the cases decided by federal district courts.

Trump has noticed. In recent weeks, the administration’s rhetoric toward the judiciary has become increasingly threatening. Senior officials, including the vice president, have floated the idea that they might not have to comply with court orders. Department of Justice lawyers have become noticeably dismissive and even rude toward the judges they appear before. Judges are increasingly concerned for their physical safety.

Trump is not just losing cases before judges nominated by other presidents. He is losing before judges he selected — judges he vouched for and insisted would apply the law faithfully and uphold the Constitution.

## Percentage of Federal Court Rulings Against the Trump Administration by Appointing Party

![partisan-appointed-judges_NL](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/partisan-appointed-judges_NL.png?width=2520&height=1528&name=partisan-appointed-judges_NL.png)

Bonica, Adam; Sen, Maya, 2025, " Common-space Measures of Judicial Ideology for Federal Judges (2024 update) ", [https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/CK3EEL](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/CK3EEL) and authors' calculations

Indeed, in his first administration, nominating conservative judges was a priority. Filling a record number of judicial vacancies with young, rock-ribbed conservatives was something he and his team bragged about. To accomplish this monumental task, Trump turned to the Federalist Society and its hyper-conservative leader, Leonard Leo.

Leo is not just any conservative. A friend of several of the most conservative Supreme Court justices, he made it his life’s mission to transform the federal judiciary into a reliable tool for a far-right agenda. Under his leadership, the Federalist Society became a well-oiled machine capable of identifying the most conservative rising lawyers and channeling them into influential positions in government — particularly the judiciary

When Don McGahn, Trump’s first White House counsel, was accused of outsourcing judicial selections to the Federalist Society, he demurred: “I’ve been a member of the Federalist Society since law school. Still am, so frankly it seems like it’s been in-sourced.” Continuing, McGahn clarified: “Seeking advice from Leonard Leo and many members of the Federalist Society is not outsourcing the judicial selection process. The fact is, we all share the same vision of the judicial role, and we welcome input from many sources.”

Now, in his second term, Trump wants it known that he does not, in fact, share the same vision for judges as Leo or his group. Trump claims he is done with the Federalist Society and Leo, whom he now calls a “bad person” and a “real sleazebag.”

The immediate reaction from the mainstream media — and even some on the left — was to categorize the rift as a genuine parting of ways. Some pointed to the Federalist Society’s lack of enthusiasm for Trump’s election denialism and the events of Jan. 6. Others claimed the former president was upset that the group was not more loyal during his criminal cases. A few even suggested it was related to Trump’s recent appointment of his henchman, Emile Bove, to the 3rd Circuit.

Each of these explanations gives both the Federalist Society and Trump more credit than they deserve.

While there are, no doubt, some members of the Federalist Society who disagreed with Trump’s efforts to overturn an election, incite an insurrection and lie about it, this was certainly not a rallying cry among its faithful. On the contrary, many prominent conservative lawyers who were outraged by Trump left the Federalist Society and formed a competing group — the Society for the Rule of Law Institute — precisely because of the Federalist Society’s continued loyalty to Trump.

By picking a fight with the Federalist Society over judges, Trump is moving the goalposts for who is considered an acceptable judicial nominee — forcing Republicans, particularly Senate Republicans, to embrace his new standard. It is no coincidence that on the same day Trump announced his disdain for the Federalist Society, his sycophantic Attorney General, Pam Bondi, sent a letter to the American Bar Association announcing it would no longer cooperate in the ABA’s process for rating judicial nominees.

Trump has made it clear that the only test for a judicial nominee is their professed loyalty to him. He is not interested in a prospective judge’s judicial philosophy or even their qualifications. All he wants to know is whether they will rule in his favor 100% of the time. If they will, he will nominate them. If not, there’s the door.

Complicating this calculation does a disservice to litigants who will appear before these future judges and to those who most rely on the rule of law. It also obscures the genuine threat to the Constitution and democracy posed by Trump and his future nominees.

We cannot allow Trump to normalize a nominating process based on loyalty to him and his agenda. We cannot permit ourselves to find common ground where subversion of the rule of law is the only common ground Trump will accept. We must oppose Trump and his judicial nominees as if democracy is at stake — because it is.

As for Leo and the Federalist Society, they will soon accommodate Trump’s latest demands and likely find a way to pay for the privilege of humiliating themselves before him. They are neither heroes nor victims of Trump. They are only enablers and collaborators.

Those of us in the pro-democracy movement cannot be fooled, must not forget and must continue to fight.
title: Don’t be fooled by Trump’s alleged break with the Federalist Society

author: Marc Elias
body:
Trump wants to be a dictator, and not just for one day. Since he stepped back into the Oval Office, nearly every day brings new examples of Trump’s commitment to authoritarianism and obsession with election denialism and subversion. From his numerous unconstitutional executive orders, through which he attempts to rule by fiat, to his public threats against political rivals and opponents, Trump has no shame in exposing his true intentions.

Most importantly, Trump has eagerly aligned himself with despots, their regimes and their values. Just look at his desire to accept a $400 million plane from the Qatari government. He’s partially motivated to turn it into Air Force One *because* it comes from a despot, not *despite* that fact. He wants to be part of their club. For Trump, the reality that accepting it would violate the Constitution is a feature, not a flaw — it brings him emotionally closer to a world where, like the royal families of the Gulf States, he can act with impunity.

This situation would be less dire if the Republican Party had not transformed into a movement that views authoritarianism as a virtue and democracy as a weakness. Curtis Yarvin, once a fringe right-wing zealot who advocates replacing U.S. democracy with a monarchy, is now seen as an influential thinker within the GOP. Donald Trump’s official store even sells merchandise for a 2028 campaign — knowing full well that if he were to seek reelection, it would be a direct violation of the U.S. Constitution’s limit of two presidential terms.

Even as they create a mythology of Trump as a man of historical destiny, his MAGA adherents remain fixated on the 2020 election because it provides glaring evidence that Trump is not invincible. It also serves as a roadmap to potentially wipe out his congressional majorities in 2026, exposing him as a fundamentally unpopular politician.

With insufficient scrutiny, the formal structures of the Republican Party — the Republican National Committee and its state affiliates — have become full-time, year-round voter suppression and election subversion machines. Bolstered by a president who cares about nothing else and a network of right-wing organizations, Republicans have nearly limitless funds to target likely Democratic voters with new barriers to voter registration and ballot counting.

Novel voter suppression techniques are the most valuable currency in the MAGA-dominated GOP, and nationwide, at every level of government, Republican officials are playing their part. Republican legislatures pass new anti-voting laws designed to disproportionately affect voters likely to oppose them. Republican election officials devise creative methods to administer elections in ways that disadvantage Democrats. Election deniers file baseless challenges and flood local election offices with conspiracy theories and hate.

Behind all these efforts is an army of Republican lawyers with unlimited budgets, advancing legal theories meant to undermine free and fair elections. In recent years, Republicans have promoted theories that would strip state courts of the power to invalidate anti-voting laws passed by GOP legislatures, empower election-denying local officials to refuse to certify election results and gut the remaining provisions of the Voting Rights Act.

While these efforts have mostly been defeated so far, those of us in the pro-democracy legal community know we must win almost every case, while the other side only needs to win a few. Most recently, these were the stakes in North Carolina, where Republicans tried to steal a state supreme court seat. In lawsuit after lawsuit, the GOP sought to disenfranchise tens of thousands of voters by applying new rules and legal standards after the election was over.

Although they ultimately failed, the risk remains. At her swearing-in, the victorious candidate, Allison Riggs, lamented, “It doesn’t have to be this way. Voters should not have to fight tooth and nail to have their lawful votes counted.”

Justice Riggs is right. We should not have to fight for democracy. But the reality is that we must.

The future of our democracy does not rest within the Republican Party. It will not reform itself as long as Trump is in charge. Meanwhile, the GOP’s assaults on democracy will continue to escalate. It will spare no expense, adhere to no norm and will not feel bound by any legal principle standing in its way.

But what about us? How committed are we to fighting for democracy today and securing free and fair elections in 2026? Rather than searching for signs of decency among Republicans, we must honestly assess our willingness to fight.

In the past, this was a matter of priorities, messaging and resources. Now, it requires all those things, plus courage and determination. At a time when Trump targets law firms, political opponents and nonprofit organizations, courage seems in short supply — but it is more crucial now than ever.

We must also possess the determination to fight as hard and as long as our Republican adversaries. They fought for six months for a single state supreme court seat, ignoring public criticism and setbacks along the way. Republicans are willing to advance a series of fringe, long-shot cases in the hopes of securing a single victory.

We will not win the battle for democracy if those in the pro-democracy movement prize caution over taking risks. We cannot permit ourselves to be deterred by the inevitable defeats and public criticism that are inevitable along the long path of restoring democracy. Now is the time for fearless leadership and bold action.

The fight for democracy is the fight of our generation. It may not be the battle we wanted, but it is the one we face. The state of our democracy and the future of our country depends on our willingness to take risks, ignore the naysayers, weather setbacks and fight fiercely for democracy and free and fair elections.

| For ways to get involved in the opposition and fight back, check out Marc’s list of 10 things you can do right now to protect free and fair elections. |
| --- |
title: Democracy’s survival depends on our willingness to fight

author: Marc Elias
body:
It’s only Tuesday, and already this week Donald Trump has said he doesn’t know if, as president, he must uphold the U.S. Constitution and has suggested imposing tariffs on movies. One reason it’s difficult to cover politics and the courts in this era is that Trump so frequently breaks the law and violates the Constitution that it is hard to keep track of everything else threatening democracy.

But every so often, we need to turn down the volume on Trump and pay attention to what his allies are doing to subvert democracy. When we do, we inevitably hear the low roar of the Republican voter suppression machine grinding away in the courts.

Even when the GOP is defeated in lower courts, Republicans routinely bring their fanciful claims and novel legal theories to the U.S. Supreme Court. We all remember how, in December 2020, Trump made a last-ditch attempt to have the Court overturn the election results. It failed.

So did the Republican effort to strip state courts of the power to enforce their own constitutions through the radical fringe “independent state legislature theory.” Most recently, Republicans failed to gut the Voting Rights Act in a case involving Alabama’s congressional districts.

But the fact that the GOP keeps losing doesn’t mean it has given up. Republicans understand that all it takes is persuading the conservative majority on the Supreme Court in one critical case to profoundly impact free and fair elections.

Savvy observers of law and politics know that even the most frivolous legal theory can gain traction — with dire consequences. They may be long-shots, but here are the current anti-voting lawsuits the right-wing is pitching to the Supreme Court for review:

### Pennsylvania Ballot Curing Challenge

The RNC and Pennsylvania Republican Party continue their obsession with disqualifying ballots in Pennsylvania. In this case, they are invoking the failed independent state legislature theory to overturn a 2024 Pennsylvania Supreme Court ruling. That decision allowed voters to cast provisional ballots on Election Day if their timely submitted mail-in ballots were disqualified due to a technical error. The RNC claims the state court “transgressed” the ordinary bounds of judicial review and “usurped” the authority of the Pennsylvania Legislature.

This is one of the most important cases to watch.

### Michigan Voting Rights Constitutional Amendments Challenge

Republican state legislators are asking the Supreme Court to review their challenge to two pro-voting constitutional amendments recently enshrined in the Michigan Constitution. A district court dismissed the case, and the 6th Circuit upheld that dismissal. Yet, the GOP is asking the Court to decide if individual state legislators can bring Election Clause claims against the executive branch, even though the legislature — then controlled by Democrats — did not challenge the amendments when they passed.

This is a longshot, but one that would harm voting in Michigan.

### Virginia Felony Disenfranchisement Challenge

Pro-voting groups are using a novel legal theory to challenge Virginia’s constitutional provision that disenfranchises people with felony convictions. The plaintiffs argue that this provision violates the Virginia Readmission Act of 1870, which only permits the state to deny the right to vote to those convicted of “common law” felonies as defined in 1870. Members of the Virginia State Board of Elections are trying to halt the case by asking the U.S. Supreme Court to determine whether courts can enforce the Readmission Act.

This is a very interesting case involving an arcane area of the law. Yet, for people with felony convictions, the consequences are very real.

### Illinois Ballot Receipt Deadline

Illinois Republican Rep. Michael Bost has asked the U.S. Supreme Court to review his challenge to the state’s ballot receipt deadline. Both a federal district court and the 7th Circuit dismissed the case for lack of standing, as Bost and two GOP presidential elector nominees failed to show any harm caused by the state’s 14-day ballot receipt window. Bost now argues that the courts erred and is asking SCOTUS to rule that he and the other plaintiffs may challenge the state law.

I would be shocked if the court took this case, but it shows the Republican’s continued obsession with mail ballot receipt deadlines.
title: The Republicans’ latest Supreme Court gambit

author: Marc Elias
body:
I usually save my commentary on the news of the week for Friday. But frankly, there has already been too much news to wait that long.

Tuesday began with the announcement that three more federal prosecutors in Manhattan had resigned over the Department of Justice‚Äôs corrupt decision to dismiss the criminal case against New York City Mayor Eric Adams. As you‚Äôll recall, the initial dismissal had already prompted several high-profile resignations, each explained in bold and pointed letters.

The latest resignations stemmed from the DOJ‚Äôs apparent insistence that the three prosecutors ‚Äúexpress regret and admit some wrongdoing‚Äù related to the office‚Äôs refusal to dismiss the case. Instead of falsely confessing, the prosecutors penned a letter calling out the impropriety of the DOJ‚Äôs demand for loyalty to the administration over professional ethics:

*The Department has decided that obedience supersedes all else, requiring us to abdicate our legal and ethical obligations in favor of directions from Washington. That is wrong.*

In contrast to these principled lawyers in New York, the government attorneys defending the illegal deportation of Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia to a Salvadoran gulag further disgraced themselves. Despite being ordered to participate in discovery, DOJ lawyers filed objections to nearly every request for information they received.

By day‚Äôs end, the judge‚Äôs frustration was evident. The court found that the government had ‚Äúevaded compliance with the court‚Äôs orders‚Äù and acted in ‚Äúbad faith.‚Äù As the Department settles in for a long fight, it must know that a finding of contempt is likely in its future.

Past administrations would have found that intolerable. But Trump relishes breaking the law. What‚Äôs more surprising is that lawyers continue to risk their reputations ‚Äî and their bar licenses ‚Äî in his service.

Meanwhile, this week has already seen important voting news.

Mississippi announced it would soon seek Supreme Court review in a Republican lawsuit challenging mail-in ballots that are postmarked by Election Day but received afterward. This has been a long-standing GOP target and the subject of a recent Trump executive order. Now it may be heading to the high court, which could issue a decision impacting ballot receipt deadlines for dozens of states ‚Äî not just Mississippi.

In North Carolina, the 4th Circuit halted a state court‚Äìordered cure process for several thousand ballots in a contested state Supreme Court race ‚Äî the election was in November, yet the Republican candidate continues to challenge his loss. Everything will remain on hold while federal courts consider whether the GOP‚Äôs attempt to undermine the election violates the U.S. Constitution.

### **Today‚Äôs Top Line**

The shift in momentum against the Trump administration is palpable. The White House is mired in scandal and internal discord. The economic outlook darkens daily, and our foreign policy is in disarray. The courts continue to hand them defeats at nearly every level, including the Supreme Court.

As the administration grows more desperate and more principled lawyers exit, it has become increasingly defiant and reckless in court. It is baiting judges to hold it in contempt. More judges will almost certainly do so. We are on the brink of a full blown constitutional crisis.

### **Today‚Äôs Heroes**

The three federal prosecutors in New York ‚Äî Celia Cohen, Andrew Rohrbach and Derek Wikstrom ‚Äî who stood up to the DOJ and resigned rather than lie about Eric Adams: ‚ÄúWe will not confess wrongdoing when there was none.‚Äù

### **Today‚Äôs Fools and Cowards**

The high-priced lawyers who still refuse to speak out for the rule of law.

### **Today‚Äôs Siren üö®**

The head of CBS‚Äô *60 Minutes* resigned yesterday, stating: ‚ÄúIt has become clear that I would not be allowed to run the show as I have always run it‚Äîto make independent decisions based on what was right for *60 Minutes*, right for the audience.‚Äù

Expect CBS to fully capitulate to Trump soon.

### **Watching This Week**

With the White House buried under bad press, watch for an outrageous stunt to divert attention. I don‚Äôt know what it will be, but I‚Äôm watching detentions, deportations and the courts.
title: It‚Äôs only Wednesday, but we already have heroes, cowards and sirens of the week

author: Marc Elias
body:
Tonight marks the first night of Passover, the Jewish holiday celebrating the exodus of Israelites from slavery in Egypt. Since I was often the youngest child at the Seder table, it usually fell on me to recite the Four Questions that begin with the familiar *Ma Nishtana*, which translates to “Why is this night different from all other nights?”

While Hanukkah was most Jewish children’s favorite holiday, in my household, it was always Pesach. I was captivated by the plagues, terrified by the image of enslaved Jews forced to make bricks without straw, and awed by the parting of the Red Sea. I took pride in Moses standing up to Pharaoh with the powerful words: “Let my people go,” and I found hope in proclaiming at the end of the meal, “Next year in Jerusalem!”

But most of all, I loved the feast — sitting around the table with family, eating special foods reserved just for this night, partaking in matzah and enjoying strange desserts made without flour. For a kid growing up in New York in the 1970s and '80s, it was joyful and exotic.

As an adult, I came to understand the broader significance of Passover. It’s the only Jewish holiday that aligns with the Christian calendar — it was, after all, the setting for Jesus’ Last Supper. And for many communities, including Black Americans, the story of the Exodus has come to symbolize a universal narrative of liberation from oppression.

This year, the holiday holds added meaning for me and many others. It is the story of a person willing to risk himself and his community to stand up to a tyrannical ruler.

The story of Moses teaches us that it's okay to suffer doubt and to question whether our efforts will succeed or fail. Even the best leaders make mistakes and are at times overcome by emotion. What matters is remaining steadfast in our commitment to what is right and having the courage to confront the powerful.

This weekend, whether you're celebrating Passover, preparing for Easter or simply taking time to relax, consider what you can do to stand up for what you know is right. It might be something as small as posting on social media. It might mean attending a peaceful protest in your community. It might even be standing up to an aspiring dictator and making clear: we are not afraid and won’t back down.

Most importantly, don’t let the scale of the challenge stop you from taking the first step.

Here are four articles to help answer your democracy questions.

###### YOUR READING LIST

- ### [‘The Most Extraordinary Attack on Voting Rights in American History’: How the SAVE Act Upends Over a Century and a Half of Protecting Voting](https://www.democracydocket.com/analysis/the-most-extraordinary-attack-on-voting-rights-in-american-history-how-the-save-act-upends-over-a-century-and-a-half-of-protecting-voting/)
- ### [SAVE Act: House Passes GOP Voting Bill That Could Disenfranchise Millions](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/house-passes-save-act-voter-suppression-law/)
- ### [What We Must Do Now To Win in 2026](https://www.democracydocket.com/opinion/what-we-must-do-now-to-win-in-2026/)
- ### [Allison Riggs: ‘This Is a Fight for the Very Soul of Democracy’](https://www.democracydocket.com/analysis/allison-riggs-this-is-a-fight-for-the-very-soul-of-democracy/)

![passover](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/passover.png?width=816&height=408&name=passover.png)
title: Why this night, and this moment, are different from all others

author: Marc Elias
body:
The greatest threat to Donald Trump’s authoritarian dream is not the courts — it is free and fair elections. While the courts can stand in the way of his twisted vision for America, they can never completely halt it. Trump knows this.

Trump operates on the assumption that there will be no political check on his power. His plan to subvert democracy depends on the subservience of a Republican-controlled Congress. His worst nightmare is a scenario in which Democrats flip one or both chambers in 2026.

It was only a matter of time before Trump turned his attention to attacking free and fair elections. Election denialism is the foundation of his movement. Election subversion is essential to maintaining his power. Voter suppression is a tool Republicans have wielded against those who do not support him.

For weeks — almost since the day he was sworn in — Trump has quietly mused about attacking elections. He referenced “election integrity” in a sweeping White House memo targeting lawyers who litigated against his administration.

At an event announcing a new Hyundai steel plant in Louisiana, Trump used the opportunity to air his grievances about lawyers and voting rules. He claimed that lawyers had behaved “very badly, very wrongly.” As a result, he declared, “our elections are very dishonest, very corrupt.”

That was merely a prelude to the executive order he and his team issued the next day. Titled *Preserving and Protecting the Integrity of American Elections*, it is a rambling document that asserts powers the president simply does not have.

It would disenfranchise millions. It seeks to restrict what ballots states can count. It purports to give DOGE unspecified powers to review state run voter rolls. No wonder vote suppressors and election deniers from coast to coast lavished it with praise.

If, as Mario Cuomo once said, “You campaign in poetry, you govern in prose,” then Donald Trump issues executive orders as performance art. Like many of his others, this order is confused, rhetorical and — in places — nonsensical. It asserts facts that are not true and claims authority he does not possess. It is not meant to be taken seriously or literally. Rather, it is the empty threat of a weak man desperate to appear strong.

The Constitution is explicit about who has the power to determine how elections are conducted:

*The Times, Places and Manner of holding Elections for Senators and Representatives, shall be prescribed in each State by the Legislature thereof; but the Congress may at any time by Law make or alter such Regulations, except as to the Places of chusing Senators.*

States have the primary role in setting election rules. Congress can override those rules by enacting laws. The president is not mentioned — because he has no independent role.

In fact, Congress has already enacted laws governing many aspects of elections. Those laws are just that — laws. They cannot be altered or overridden by a piece of paper in a fancy folder, no matter how forcefully Trump signs it with a Sharpie.

Calling it an executive order may mean something to him, but legally, it is irrelevant. Neither the Constitution nor the law cares about his feelings.

Trump’s advisors undoubtedly know this and have likely explained it to him. But Trump doesn’t care. He believes that by signing an outrageous document filled with threats and bluster, he can intimidate state and local officials, lawyers, judges and voters. He is counting on us being too scared to fight back.

He is also counting on voters not caring enough to exercise their rights. By making the process seem complicated, contentious and dangerous, he hopes to bully the election system into submission.

We cannot let that happen.

I have already announced that my team will sue to block this executive order and any other attempts to undermine free and fair elections. Our track record proves that we litigate aggressively — and when we do, we typically win.

That is why Trump is attacking me. It is why he went to the Department of Justice to call me a “radical” trying to “turn America into a corrupt, communist, third-world country.” It is why he is attempting to destroy my former law firm, where I defeated his legal team more than 60 times in 2020. And it is why he singled me out in his recent White House memorandum.

I don’t care what Donald Trump says about me. I wear his scorn as a badge of honor.

But one lawyer — or even a thousand lawyers — won’t solve this problem alone. It will take the civic courage of thousands of leaders and millions of voters.

This executive order is bad — cartoonishly so. But it is also a wake-up call for those who believe the danger of election subversion has passed.

To the contrary, it has only just begun.

And so has our fight.
title: Trump’s latest executive order is a sham—and a warning

author: Marc Elias
body:
In a week filled with critical developments in democracy, one story stood out. Ten days ago, Donald Trump targeted a specific law firm ‚Äî my former firm ‚Äî for political retribution via a blatantly unconstitutional executive order. The firm‚Äôs "offense"? Representing Trump‚Äôs political opponents, defeating him in court and advancing diversity policies he opposes.

The immediate question was what Big Law would do. Would they stand up to the president or would they stay silent? Most importantly, would any firm be willing to represent the targeted law firm and risk their own retaliation?

While most major law firms cowered in fear, refusing even to condemn the action, one firm ‚Äî Williams & Connolly ‚Äî took the case and fought back aggressively. As one of the nation‚Äôs most formidable litigation firms, their presence in the case spoke volumes even as their legal arguments landed blow after blow.

Their legal brief began by calling Trump‚Äôs executive action ‚Äúan unconstitutional assault on ‚Ä¶ the legal profession, and the rule of law.‚Äù It ended with 14 individual lawyers from the firm signing their names. If you want to see what courage in the legal profession looks like, just glance at that [**page**](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/gov.uscourts.dcd_.278290.1.0.pdf) ‚Äî you‚Äôll see it in its purest form.

As expected, after hearing the arguments, the court temporarily blocked Trump‚Äôs unlawful executive order while the case proceeds. Before issuing the order, however, the judge acknowledged the extraordinary bravery of the attorneys who took on the case: ‚ÄúI have enormous respect for Williams & Connolly and enormous respect for them taking this case when not every law firm would.‚Äù

Amen.

| Restricting Birthright Citizenship | 10 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have blocked this action. |
| --- | --- |
| Federal Funding Freeze | 5 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have blocked most efforts to freeze federal funds. |
| Establishing DOGE | 5 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have yet to rule , so the action is in effect . |
| Granting DOGE Agency Access | 12 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have blocked DOGE‚Äôs access to the Departments of the Treasury and Education and the United States African Development Foundation. Courts have yet to rule in cases related to DOGE‚Äôs access to the IRS and USAID so those actions are in effect . |
| Unlawfully Dismissing Political Appointees | 7 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have reinstated the chair of the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB); a National Labor Relations Board member and a Federal Labor Relations Authority member. |
| Targeting Federal Employees and Civil Service | 9 lawsuits have been filed against this action. Status: Courts have blocked actions firing federal workers at several agencies including the Departments of Agriculture, Defense, Energy, Interior, Treasury and Veterans Affairs. An agreement stemming from litigation has blocked some efforts targeting FBI employees. |
| Dismantling Agencies (CFPB, USAID, Dept. of Education) | 6 lawsuits have been filed against these efforts. Status: Courts have blocked some efforts to dismantle the USAID . Courts have yet to rule on efforts to dismantle the Department of Education. |

| The Weekly Top Line Big Law is no more courageous than any other corporate institution. These firms stand up for democracy when it is convenient and popular. But the moment economic risk is involved, they retreat in fear. Williams & Connolly is a large firm, though not among the very largest. While it has corporate ties, it is best known for its aggressive, bet-the-company litigation rather than its client base. This positioning made it well-suited to take on the challenges of an unhinged Trump administration. We‚Äôve seen this pattern again and again ‚Äî whether in media, business or public institutions. The largest entities are failing us, while those with the independence and reputation to resist government pressure are rising to the occasion. Win of the Week This one is easy. On Wednesday, federal judge Beryl Howell granted a temporary restraining order against Trump‚Äôs administration, blocking enforcement of his retaliatory executive order targeting Perkins Coie. Howell, one of the smartest federal judges on the bench, had little patience for the government‚Äôs weak arguments and acted swiftly and decisively. Kudos to her. Heroes of the Week Here are the fourteen lawyers who put their names on the line in defense of another law firm, the rule of law and the U.S. Constitution. Heroes, one and all: Dane H. Butswinkas F. Lane Heard III Christopher N. Manning Ryan T. Scarborough Malachi B. Jones Charles Davant IV David S. Kurtzer-Ellenbogen Jesse T. Smallwood Amy M. Saharia Matthew B. Nicholson Carol J. Pruski Charles L. McCloud Krystal C. Durham Eden Schiffmann | The Weekly Top Line Big Law is no more courageous than any other corporate institution. These firms stand up for democracy when it is convenient and popular. But the moment economic risk is involved, they retreat in fear. Williams & Connolly is a large firm, though not among the very largest. While it has corporate ties, it is best known for its aggressive, bet-the-company litigation rather than its client base. This positioning made it well-suited to take on the challenges of an unhinged Trump administration. We‚Äôve seen this pattern again and again ‚Äî whether in media, business or public institutions. The largest entities are failing us, while those with the independence and reputation to resist government pressure are rising to the occasion. Win of the Week This one is easy. On Wednesday, federal judge Beryl Howell granted a temporary restraining order against Trump‚Äôs administration, blocking enforcement of his retaliatory executive order targeting Perkins Coie. Howell, one of the smartest federal judges on the bench, had little patience for the government‚Äôs weak arguments and acted swiftly and decisively. Kudos to her. Heroes of the Week Here are the fourteen lawyers who put their names on the line in defense of another law firm, the rule of law and the U.S. Constitution. Heroes, one and all: Dane H. Butswinkas F. Lane Heard III Christopher N. Manning Ryan T. Scarborough Malachi B. Jones Charles Davant IV David S. Kurtzer-Ellenbogen Jesse T. Smallwood Amy M. Saharia Matthew B. Nicholson Carol J. Pruski Charles L. McCloud Krystal C. Durham Eden Schiffmann |
| --- | --- |
| The Weekly Top Line Big Law is no more courageous than any other corporate institution. These firms stand up for democracy when it is convenient and popular. But the moment economic risk is involved, they retreat in fear. Williams & Connolly is a large firm, though not among the very largest. While it has corporate ties, it is best known for its aggressive, bet-the-company litigation rather than its client base. This positioning made it well-suited to take on the challenges of an unhinged Trump administration. We‚Äôve seen this pattern again and again ‚Äî whether in media, business or public institutions. The largest entities are failing us, while those with the independence and reputation to resist government pressure are rising to the occasion. Win of the Week This one is easy. On Wednesday, federal judge Beryl Howell granted a temporary restraining order against Trump‚Äôs administration, blocking enforcement of his retaliatory executive order targeting Perkins Coie. Howell, one of the smartest federal judges on the bench, had little patience for the government‚Äôs weak arguments and acted swiftly and decisively. Kudos to her. Heroes of the Week Here are the fourteen lawyers who put their names on the line in defense of another law firm, the rule of law and the U.S. Constitution. Heroes, one and all: Dane H. Butswinkas F. Lane Heard III Christopher N. Manning Ryan T. Scarborough Malachi B. Jones Charles Davant IV David S. Kurtzer-Ellenbogen Jesse T. Smallwood Amy M. Saharia Matthew B. Nicholson Carol J. Pruski Charles L. McCloud Krystal C. Durham Eden Schiffmann |  |
| Fool and Cowards of the Week When federal prosecutor Hagen Scotten resigned rather than file a motion to dismiss the case against New York City Mayor Eric Adams, he told his superior: ‚ÄúI expect you will eventually find someone who is enough of a fool, or enough of a coward, to file your motion. But it was never going to be me.‚Äù Since then, a parade of DOJ lawyers has lined up in case after case, eager to earn those titles. Right on cue, two career DOJ lawyers entered appearances to defend Trump‚Äôs law firm executive order, making them this week‚Äôs Cowards of the Week . The courtroom argument fell to Attorney General Pam Bondi‚Äôs chief of staff, Chad Mizelle. Typically, someone in Mizelle‚Äôs position does not argue any cases in court ‚Äî especially not motions for a temporary restraining order. In fact, I have never seen someone so senior take on such a role or get so totally humiliated by a judge. That earns him the title of Fool of the Week . The Week‚Äôs Siren üö® All of this is happening, and we are still less than three months into Trump‚Äôs second term. The next three years and nine months will be challenging, but we must all remain engaged and up for the fight. Watching Next Week Trump promised retribution, and we are seeing it unfold daily. This week, he targeted lawyers. Next week, it will be another group he seeks to intimidate. Upon signing the Declaration of Independence, Benjamin Franklin reportedly said: ‚ÄúWe must, indeed, all hang together or, most assuredly, we shall all hang separately.‚Äù That remains sound advice for anyone standing against a despot. | Fool and Cowards of the Week When federal prosecutor Hagen Scotten resigned rather than file a motion to dismiss the case against New York City Mayor Eric Adams, he told his superior: ‚ÄúI expect you will eventually find someone who is enough of a fool, or enough of a coward, to file your motion. But it was never going to be me.‚Äù Since then, a parade of DOJ lawyers has lined up in case after case, eager to earn those titles. Right on cue, two career DOJ lawyers entered appearances to defend Trump‚Äôs law firm executive order, making them this week‚Äôs Cowards of the Week . The courtroom argument fell to Attorney General Pam Bondi‚Äôs chief of staff, Chad Mizelle. Typically, someone in Mizelle‚Äôs position does not argue any cases in court ‚Äî especially not motions for a temporary restraining order. In fact, I have never seen someone so senior take on such a role or get so totally humiliated by a judge. That earns him the title of Fool of the Week . The Week‚Äôs Siren üö® All of this is happening, and we are still less than three months into Trump‚Äôs second term. The next three years and nine months will be challenging, but we must all remain engaged and up for the fight. Watching Next Week Trump promised retribution, and we are seeing it unfold daily. This week, he targeted lawyers. Next week, it will be another group he seeks to intimidate. Upon signing the Declaration of Independence, Benjamin Franklin reportedly said: ‚ÄúWe must, indeed, all hang together or, most assuredly, we shall all hang separately.‚Äù That remains sound advice for anyone standing against a despot. |
| Fool and Cowards of the Week When federal prosecutor Hagen Scotten resigned rather than file a motion to dismiss the case against New York City Mayor Eric Adams, he told his superior: ‚ÄúI expect you will eventually find someone who is enough of a fool, or enough of a coward, to file your motion. But it was never going to be me.‚Äù Since then, a parade of DOJ lawyers has lined up in case after case, eager to earn those titles. Right on cue, two career DOJ lawyers entered appearances to defend Trump‚Äôs law firm executive order, making them this week‚Äôs Cowards of the Week . The courtroom argument fell to Attorney General Pam Bondi‚Äôs chief of staff, Chad Mizelle. Typically, someone in Mizelle‚Äôs position does not argue any cases in court ‚Äî especially not motions for a temporary restraining order. In fact, I have never seen someone so senior take on such a role or get so totally humiliated by a judge. That earns him the title of Fool of the Week . The Week‚Äôs Siren üö® All of this is happening, and we are still less than three months into Trump‚Äôs second term. The next three years and nine months will be challenging, but we must all remain engaged and up for the fight. Watching Next Week Trump promised retribution, and we are seeing it unfold daily. This week, he targeted lawyers. Next week, it will be another group he seeks to intimidate. Upon signing the Declaration of Independence, Benjamin Franklin reportedly said: ‚ÄúWe must, indeed, all hang together or, most assuredly, we shall all hang separately.‚Äù That remains sound advice for anyone standing against a despot. |  |
title: The Week: 14 courageous lawyers, 1 smart judge, 2 cowards and a fool

author: Marc Elias
body:
If you subscribe to *Democracy Docket*, chances are you are an avid consumer of news. Yet, even for those of us who diligently pay attention, it can be difficult to stay up to date on Donald Trump’s latest attacks on democracy.

Part of this is a failure of the legacy media itself—much of which is now more accurately described as oligarch-controlled media. However, it is also part of a deliberate Republican strategy to spread lies and disinformation about what is happening.

This is often referred to as the “flood the zone” strategy. It’s worth revisiting exactly what Steve Bannon said when he coined the phrase in 2018: *The Democrats don’t matter. The real opposition is the media. And the way to deal with them is to flood the zone with shit.*

I started *Democracy Docket* —and this newsletter in particular—to combat exactly this strategy. Each Saturday, I highlight the most important news of the week that may have been buried under the flood of misinformation the GOP has intentionally released.

This week is a prime example of why this effort is so necessary. On the one hand, the GOP wants to portray Trump as an all-powerful president imposing his will on a fearful and dispirited government. It is true that he is taking outrageous actions. It is also true that Republicans in Congress have been intimidated into serving as his rubber stamp.

However, it is not true that he is having his way. The reality is that government workers are standing up. And he is losing most of the lawsuits challenging his illegal actions. Plaintiffs, their lawyers and federal judges are not backing down. Instead, they are upholding the rule of law. It’s not a perfect system, but the news from the courts is more good than bad.

Another significant development is worth noting: the GOP’s election subversion war machine is revving up—more emboldened than ever. But it, too, is losing in court. Just this week, two different courts—one a state trial court, the other a federal appeals court—handed Republicans and their allies stinging defeats.

I’m not saying all the news is good or that democracy isn’t under immense strain. But I am saying there are signs of hope, and we must embrace them.

###### YOUR READING LIST

- ### [SCOTUS Hands Trump His First Legal Victory of Second Term](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/supreme-court-trump-first-victory-second-term/)
- ### [Voter Suppression Laws Remain Blocked in Arizona](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/voter-suppression-laws-remain-blocked-in-arizona/)
- ### [Judge Extends Halt on Trump’s Firing of Head of Watchdog Agency For Federal Workers](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/judge-extends-halt-trump-firing-watchdog-agency-headfor-federal-workers/)
- ### [Judge Rules Trump Officials Must Testify in DOGE Data Lawsuit](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/judge-trump-officials-testify-doge-data-lawsuit/)
- ### [Arizona Court Dismisses Right-Wing Group’s Election Administration Lawsuit](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/arizona-court-dismisses-right-wing-groups-election-administration-lawsuit/)

Trump might not be having it his way, but Bode certainly is!

![Bode (4)](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/Bode%20(4).png?width=1200&height=600&name=Bode%20(4).png)
title: Signs of Hope in a Flooded Zone

author: Marc Elias
body:
With the flurry of executive orders and the chaos Elon Musk and others are creating, it is easy to lose sight of the fact that the only consistent through line to Donald Trump’s politics is election denialism and voter suppression.

Tariffs are negotiable, government agencies can come and go, even political enemies can even score pardons if they offer the right deal. But no one — I mean no one — can be in good standing in Trump’s GOP if they do not insist on denying the outcome of elections he has lost and advocate for suppressing the voting rights of citizens who did not vote for him. Period.

That is why Republicans in Congress have prioritized enacting the so-called SAVE Act. The Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act would seriously undermine voting rights under the false pretense of preventing nearly non-existent instances of noncitizen voting in federal elections.

Among its core provisions, it would require those seeking to register to vote to provide documentary proof of citizenship. For most people born in the United States this would require either a passport or certified birth certificate.

![1-required-to-vote](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/1-required-to-vote.png?width=1200&height=444&name=1-required-to-vote.png)

Plenty of commentators have noted the difficulties this would pose for younger and less affluent voters who more often lack passports. Others have noted the discrimination married women who adopt their husband’s surname will face, since their birth certificate name will not match their married name. And, of course, there is the fact that this will make voter registration other than in person in a government office either very difficult or impossible.

Simply put, the SAVE Act is a voter suppression bill aimed at making it harder for many Democratic constituencies to vote and easier for the GOP to cheat. But, under Trump’s scheme, that isn’t the worst of it.

Among his first executive orders was purporting to end birthright citizenship — the right to automatic citizenship for any person born in the United States regardless of the status of their parents. Notwithstanding the clear text of the U.S. Constitution, Trump has claimed the power to replace birthright citizenship with a more restrictive regime whereby many people born in the U.S. would not be citizens if their parents were not.

![2-Trumps-first-EOs](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/2-Trumps-first-EOs.jpeg?width=2500&height=1259&name=2-Trumps-first-EOs.jpeg)

The result for voting would be clear. U.S. birth certificates would no longer serve as conclusive proof of citizenship. Citizens seeking to register to vote would lose a critical piece of documentary proof of citizenship needed to satisfy the requirements of the SAVE Act, and state laws like it.

This is no oversight by Trump and the GOP. Several of the lawsuits challenging Trump’s executive order make exactly this point. Yet, the Trump administration has offered no assurances that its unconstitutional effort to strip citizenship would not ultimately be a tool to unconstitutionally strip citizens of the right to vote.

![3-no-birth-certificate-no-voting](https://member.democracydocket.com/hs-fs/hubfs/3-no-birth-certificate-no-voting.png?width=1200&height=444&name=3-no-birth-certificate-no-voting.png)

But that isn’t the only action Trump took to undermine voting in advance of the 2026 elections. He also rescinded Joe Biden’s 2021 pro-voting executive order that promoted increasing voter registrations nationwide. And he promptly put on leave employees within the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency whose jobs are helping to secure elections from foreign threats and disinformation.

Meanwhile, the Republican Party is continuing to push its anti-voting agenda in court. The RNC is again asking the U.S. Supreme Court to hear a case involving the fringe independent state legislature theory, this time out of Pennsylvania. You may recall, the Republican Attorney General in Montana recently failed in a similar effort involving a series of voter suppression laws struck down in that state.

Perhaps worst of all, Republican state Supreme Court candidate Jefferson Griffin is still trying to steal an election in North Carolina by throwing out more than 60,000 lawful votes. A state trial court recently [**rejected**](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/north-carolina-judge-rejects-gop-state-supreme-court-candidates-bid-to-throw-out-60k-ballots/) his challenge, but he has since appealed that decision.

Finally, while most eyes are on Washington, DC, Republican legislatures around the country are meeting to consider new draconian anti-voting laws. Here are just a few examples.

- The Wisconsin state legislature has [**approved**](https://apnews.com/article/wisconsin-voter-id-election-photo-f6a74b9c83ef0fb147e67489a9df1274) a ballot measure to enshrine a voter ID requirement in the state constitution. The proposed amendment will appear on the ballot for the April 1 election.
- Similarly, Maine Republicans are pushing a [**voter ID constitutional amendment**](https://voteridforme.com/files/photo_id_for_voting_petition_final.pdf) that would make sweeping changes to election procedure. In addition to voter ID requirements, the proposal would restrict absentee ballot processes, limit drop-box use, and introduce other hurdles to the right to vote.
- The North Carolina state legislature is [**considering**](https://www.carolinajournal.com/new-bill-would-reduce-early-voting-days-in-nc/) a bill to shorten the state’s early voting period from 17 days to 8 days, while [**Alaska Republicans**](https://gov.alaska.gov/governor-dunleavy-introduces-three-key-bills-to-advance-alaskas-priorities-on-day-two-of-legislative-session/) are working to slash the last five days of early voting before election day.
- The Wyoming state legislature considered a bill that would have banned the use of electronic voting machines. It would’ve made Wyoming the only state in the nation to require hand-counting all ballots.
- In order to circumvent Governor Hobbs, Arizona lawmakers are [**rapidly advancing**](https://www.votebeat.org/arizona/2025/02/12/election-results-speed-faster-bill-compromise-katie-hobbs-veto-threat/) a ballot measure that restricts ballot return and creates onerous requirements to remain on the Active Early Voter List.
- Indiana lawmakers are pushing bills that would make it harder to [**register**](https://indianacapitalchronicle.com/2025/01/28/elections-bill-would-strike-down-student-ids-as-voting-option/) to vote, [**stay**](https://iga.in.gov/legislative/2025/bills/house/1679/details) on the voter rolls, and [**cast**](https://indianacapitalchronicle.com/2025/02/03/measure-halving-early-voting-period-heads-to-senate-floor/) early in-person ballots.
- Elections officials in [**Iowa**](https://www.desmoinesregister.com/story/news/politics/2025/02/03/secretary-of-state-boots-183000-iowans-off-voter-rolls-for-inactivity/78186958007/) and Arizona’s most populous [**county**](https://www.fox10phoenix.com/news/maricopa-county-removes-voters-from-registration-list-heres-why) have removed thousands allegedly inactive voters from the rolls as a new election cycle begins.
- In good news, [**Mississippi**](https://mississippitoday.org/2025/02/05/measures-allowing-former-felons-to-regain-voting-rights-clear-house-committees/) and [**Virginia**](https://boltsmag.org/virginia-advances-measure-to-end-lifetime-ban-on-voting/) lawmakers have proposed reforms to their states’ stringent voting rights restorations process for individuals with previous felony convictions

Yes, we are watching Donald Trump attack democracy by undermining governmental institutions and violating the law and Constitution. But we are also watching something else that might be even bigger. We are watching the party he leads attack the right to vote across in a multitude of ways that are not always easy to detect.

That is the current state of democracy.
title: The state of democracy: Watching Republicans attack the right to vote

author: Marc Elias
body:
Firings in the federal government have dominated the news recently. Since taking office, Donald Trump’s administration has fired government inspectors general, federal prosecutors, FBI agents and national security officials. It has pressured many others to resign or face the consequences. It recently made a near government-wide offer to career civil servants — get months of paid severance to leave your employment now.

While Republicans have looked the other way, this government purge has otherwise been widely condemned as an affront to the constitution and rule of law. Critics have pointed out the unfairness of firing career officials for merely following the law and established policy. They have rightfully worried about what the loss of expertise will mean for the smooth operations of the government moving forward.

But Trump is not trying to shrink the size of the federal government’s workforce to save money. He is not a fiscal hawk or a conservative. As Steve Bannon, the keeper of MAGA’s core ideology, recently said about himself; “I’m a hard-core populist. I’m a hard-core nationalist. I’m not a conservative. I think conservatives are [expletive].”

The most dangerous part of Trump’s plan for the federal workforce is not who leaves, it is who comes in. Call it Trump’s great replacement theory. If successful, it has the potential to weaponize the government against his political opponents and undermine our democracy for years to come. And it is already underway.

When he became president, Ronald Reagan adopted the maxim that “personnel is policy.” Conservatives of that era understood that even the most powerful president could only extend his reach so far. To ensure the administration’s policies were implemented, it was critical to put the right people in places of authority.

Back then, it was carried out through political appointees that shared Reagan’s brand of anti-Washington conservatism. It meant appointing tax-cutting conservatives and defense hawks to key posts. It meant a Department of Justice that focused on drug crimes and an interior department that exploited, rather than preserved, public lands.

The problem with Trump is that he has no governing philosophy. He will adopt and change policies based on what he believes is best for him. He has no allies he won’t betray. He has no convictions he won’t reverse. For him all politics is personal and thus personnel is personal.

We have seen this in his early cabinet nominations. Pam Bondi was chosen to head the Department of Justice because she proved a loyal election denier with a track record of saying and doing what Trump wants. Kash Patel is such a sycophant that he published a book that literally contained an enemies list as well as children’s books in which Trump is the King.

He found the one man on Wall Street willing to shill for his economy-killing tariffs. He appointed Marco Rubio secretary of state because he had proven himself willing to say or do anything Trump asked. In a court of jesters, Little Marco was the most eager to humiliate himself for Trump’s pleasure.

This would be bad enough if Trump was satisfied with a band of misfits running departments and agencies while supplicant billionaires paid for the privilege of bowing down and begging for Trump’s attention and approval. But it is worse. Much worse.

To execute his planned revenge, he needs the cooperation of FBI agents willing to disregard their oaths. To prosecute his enemies, he needs line prosecutors eager to disregard legal ethics and the evidence to indict his political opponents. To be the authoritarian he aspires to be, he needs all branches of the federal government — large and small — to reward his supporters and punish his detractors.

This is why he wants good prosecutors to resign. This is why he wants to fire FBI agents who follow the law. Because, to execute his reign of vengeance, he needs a federal workforce that is willing to carry out his orders.

Some, particularly those who stay, will be motivated by fear of losing their jobs. They will compromise themselves little by little. Others, who fill the vacancies, will be eager to display loyalty and fealty to the man who they think can aid their careers. But most will be the silent so-called good people that Martin Luthur King Jr. warned us about. They will justify their immoral actions by saying they were just following orders.

There will be some who resist — who insist on following the law and serving the American people rather than a one-man dictatorship. We have already seen examples of such heroism in the FBI, Treasury and USAID. Over time, however, the righteous will be weeded out or worn down.

We are at a critical moment in this fight for democracy. Trump’s great replacement has started but it has not taken root. It is now that it must be defeated. That will require civic courage by those in government and outside.

For those in government, stand tall and do not give into illegal orders or actions. For those outside of government, we must shine a light on these illegal actions every moment of every day. I expect little from the oligarchs and their media enablers. Independent media will necessarily have to shoulder the load, as it has already proven itself capable of doing.

Most importantly, the legal profession needs to step forward to fight against these illegal efforts. Already groups like Democracy Forward are suing and winning. But they cannot do this alone. Big law firms need to pitch in, rather than hide.

Finally, the courts will once again need to protect the Constitution and rule of law from a lawless president who has ignored his oath once again. So far, they have held. Trump is losing in court. For democracy’s sake, let’s hope that continues.

Here are my tips for the week.

###### **NEW LAWSUITS**

### **Democracy in court**

If you want to doom scroll, pay attention to what Elon Musk is doing. If you want to get angry, pay attention to OMB and the White House. But, if you want to know what is happening to democracy, pay attention to the courts. There will be a lot of litigation this week across the democracy spectrum — from Trump’s executive orders to agency actions, to voting and elections. Get used to courts being the central players for the next few years — at least.

###### **TRUMP’S CABINET**

### **Senate nominations and confirmations**

I have been on record saying that every Trump nominee will be confirmed. There may be a stray Republican here or there that votes no — but never enough of them to defeat one. This is a well-practiced game in Washington, D.C. — allowing a vulnerable Republican the ability to look independent from Trump while knowing it won’t make a difference. Though Gabbard looks to be in trouble, I am sticking to my initial prediction.

###### **MUSK IN CHARGE**

### **Congress shall make no law**

I cannot remember a president who came into office with majorities in both Houses of Congress who had zero legislative agenda. So far Speaker Johnson and Leader Thune are mere props as Trump ignores the role of Congress to, well, do anything at all. With Elon Musk seemingly playing the role of appropriator in chief, it is shocking to see Congress have zero interest in how their spending bills are being ignored. Expect this to continue.
title: My tip sheet: Trump’s great replacement
